rationale_for_baseline_tier: Classic pityriasis rosea is benign and self-limiting; the main intervention is reassurance and gentle skin care (T0). Secondary syphilis (palm/sole/mucosal involvement or risk history) routes to GP/serology (T1); it is not an emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rash spreading to the palms or soles, mouth or genital sores, or a relevant sexual-risk exposure
escalation_edges.rationale: These suggest secondary syphilis needing serology and treatment.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Strong reassurance about the benign, non-contagious nature; give a clear syphilis safety-net.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a harmless viral rash that fades over 6–8 weeks. Use gentle skin care, a moisturiser and an antihistamine for the itch, and avoid harsh soaps and hot showers.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care with telehealth/GP follow-up; GP + serology if palms/soles/mucosa involved
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Routine — not an emergency; GP for palm/sole/mucosal involvement.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Classic pityriasis rosea; secondary syphilis screened negative (no palm/sole/mucosal involvement); reassurance + emollient + antihistamine; serology if features change.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring without a secondary-syphilis screen/serology safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Secondary syphilis is mislabelled as pityriasis rosea and goes untreated.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No safety-net for evolving palm/sole/mucosal involvement.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a classic benign pityriasis rosea to ED — unnecessary.
